q: In a paper you see Σᵢ wᵢxᵢ. The direct Python equivalent?
w: Σ of pairwise products is exactly a dot product. cumsum keeps partial sums; outer builds an n×n matrix.

q: Likelihoods are products ∏ᵢ pᵢ. Why optimize the log instead?
o: Products of many small probabilities underflow; log turns ∏ into Σ with the same argmax
Logs force probabilities to sum to 1
w: log is monotonic, so the maximizer is unchanged — and 1,000 probabilities multiplied is numerically ~0.

q: argmaxₓ f(x) returns…
o: The x where f peaks — not the peak value
w: max gives the height; argmax gives the location. Classifiers predict argmax over class probabilities.

q: For θ ∈ ℝᵈ, ∇L(θ) is…
w: One slope per parameter. The d×d matrix of second derivatives is the Hessian.

q: ‖w‖² appearing in a loss (ridge) means…
w: Σ|wᵢ| is L1/lasso, which drives weights to exact zero; counting nonzeros is L0.

q: 𝟙[x > 0] evaluates to…
w: 'x if positive else 0' is ReLU — related but different. Indicators turn logic into arithmetic so it can live inside a Σ.

q: X is n×d. The shape of XᵀX is…
w: (d×n)(n×d) = d×d — the feature Gram matrix at the heart of OLS: β̂ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy.

q: ⊙ between two same-shape vectors means…
w: FiLM's γ(s) ⊙ h is exactly this: each feature scaled by its own γᵢ.

q: Your PD model has β = 0.7 on DTI. Per unit of DTI, what increases by 0.7?
w: Odds multiply by e^0.7 ≈ 2×. The probability change depends on where you sit on the S-curve — steepest near PD = 0.5.
z = 0 means evidence is perfectly balanced — a coin flip.

q: Logistic regression's decision boundary in feature space is…
w: PD = 0.5 ⟺ β·x = 0, a hyperplane. The S-curve lives in probability space, not feature space. Frequent interview trap.

q: Why model log-odds (not probability) as the linear part?
o: Log-odds range over (−∞, ∞), matching what a linear function produces
w: A line outputs any real number; probability must live in (0,1). The logit link resolves the mismatch.

q: Odds of default are 3:1. PD = ?
w: p = odds/(1+odds) = 3/4. Odds ↔ probability conversion should be automatic in interviews.

q: You rescale income from ₹ to ₹ lakhs (÷10⁵) and refit. Its coefficient…
w: β·x must produce the same log-odds: shrink x, β grows to compensate. Predictions and AUC are untouched.

q: σ′ = σ(1−σ) peaks at 0.25. Why does this matter in deep nets?
o: Each sigmoid layer multiplies gradients by ≤ 0.25 — vanishing gradients
w: Ten saturated sigmoid layers ⇒ gradient scaled by ~0.25¹⁰ ≈ 10⁻⁶. Why ReLU and LayerNorm exist.

q: Add +10 to every logit before softmax. The probabilities…
w: Shift invariance: e^(z+c) factors out of numerator and denominator. Only gaps between logits matter.

q: Production softmax code subtracts max(z) before exp. Purpose?
w: e^800 overflows to inf. Subtracting the max keeps exponents ≤ 0 — a free trick thanks to shift invariance.

q: Temperature T → 0 in softmax(z/T) gives…
o: One-hot on the largest logit — argmax
w: Tiny T blows up the gaps; the leader takes everything. T → ∞ flattens to uniform. Same lever as greedy vs creative LLM sampling.

q: Why divide by √d in attention?
o: Dot-product variance grows with dimension; scaling keeps softmax out of saturation
To match Q and K sizes
w: q·k sums d variance contributions ⇒ std ∝ √d. Unscaled, softmax goes one-hot and gradients through it die.

q: In self-attention, Q, K, V come from…
o: Three learned linear projections of the same input
w: Same input X, three learned matrices: XW_Q, XW_K, XW_V. 'Self' = queries and keys come from one sequence.

q: The attention weights for one query token always…
o: Sum to 1 — each row is a softmax
w: Each output is a convex combination — a weighted average — of value vectors. Nothing more exotic than that.

q: The gradient of softmax + cross-entropy w.r.t. the logits is famously…
w: Predicted minus true — push down what you over-believed. This clean form is why the pairing is universal.

q: The gradient points toward…
o: Steepest increase — which is why we subtract it
w: ∇L points uphill; θ − η∇L walks downhill. It knows nothing about minima — only the local slope.

q: Learning rate too large — the classic symptom:
w: You repeatedly overshoot the valley floor and climb the far wall. Recreate it in the ∇ tab: η > 1, press ×10.

q: The 'stochastic' in SGD refers to…
o: Estimating the gradient on a random minibatch — noisy but cheap
w: Bonus: that noise helps hop out of shallow local minima and saddle points. The imperfection is a feature.

q: Why standardize features before gradient descent (trees don't care)?
o: Uneven scales create stretched loss valleys — GD zigzags slowly
It moves the optimum somewhere better
w: Income in ₹ vs age in years ⇒ a canyon-shaped loss surface. Trees split on order, so monotone rescaling changes nothing for them.

q: Convexity buys you…
o: Any local minimum is the global minimum
w: Logistic loss is convex — your PD fit can't get trapped. Deep nets surrender this guarantee and survive anyway.

q: Adam differs from vanilla SGD mainly by…
o: Per-parameter adaptive step sizes from gradient moment estimates
w: It tracks running means of gradients (momentum) and squared gradients (scale), giving each weight its own effective η.

q: Early stopping is regularization because…
o: It halts before the model memorizes training noise, using validation loss as referee
w: Train loss falls forever; validation loss U-turns. Stop at the bottom of the U. Cheapest regularizer in existence.

q: EL = PD × LGD × EAD is, in notation terms…
w: Expected loss is E[L] over default scenarios. Unexpected loss (capital) is a high quantile of the same distribution.

q: E[aX + b] = ?
w: Linearity of expectation — holds even for dependent variables. Variance differs: Var(aX+b) = a²Var(X); b vanishes.

q: Var(X) in expectations:
w: Mean of squares minus square of mean. If they were equal, X would be a constant.

q: X and Y independent ⟺
w: Independence implies zero covariance, but zero covariance does NOT imply independence — a favorite follow-up.

q: A default flag fires with 90% sensitivity, but only 2% of loans default. P(default | flag) depends critically on…
w: With rare events, even a strong signal yields mostly false alarms — precision can land under 20%. Run the 2% prior through Bayes.

q: E[X] for one fair die roll:
w: Σ x·p = 21/6 = 3.5. The expectation needn't be an achievable outcome — it's a balance point, not a prediction.

q: Within μ ± 2σ of a Gaussian lies about…
w: 68 / 95 / 99.7 for 1σ / 2σ / 3σ. Why '2-sigma event' is shorthand for roughly 1-in-20.

q: A z-score of −2.5 on FICO means…
w: z = (x−μ)/σ — distance from center in units of spread. Standardization makes any two features comparable.

q: Why squared error (x−μ)² rather than |x−μ| across so much of statistics?
o: Smooth and differentiable everywhere, and it's the Gaussian MLE
w: Minimizing squared error ⟺ MLE under Gaussian noise. The mean minimizes Σ(x−c)²; the median minimizes Σ|x−c|.

q: Mortgage losses are 'heavy-tailed' means…
o: Extreme losses occur far more often than a Gaussian predicts
w: A Gaussian assigns ~zero probability to 2008. This single fact is why stress testing — your day job — exists.

q: The CLT says sums/averages of many independent effects tend toward…
o: A normal distribution, whatever each effect's shape
w: Aggregation manufactures Gaussians. Caveat: fails under heavy tails or strong dependence — see 2008, again.

q: Prefer the median over the mean when…
o: The distribution is skewed or outlier-prone, e.g. income
w: One billionaire drags a neighborhood's mean income; the median doesn't move. Robustness vs algebraic convenience.

q: Cross-entropy punishes hardest:
o: Confident wrong predictions — −log p → ∞ as p → 0 on the truth
w: PD = 0.001 on a loan that defaults costs −log(0.001) ≈ 6.9 — vs 0.69 for an honest 'don't know' at 0.5.

q: KL(P‖Q) = 0 exactly when…
o: P and Q overlap at all
w: Zero iff identical, never negative (Gibbs' inequality). Any belief differing from reality costs surprise.

q: KL(P‖Q) vs KL(Q‖P):
o: Generally different — KL is asymmetric, so not a true distance
w: Direction matters: KL(P‖Q) punishes Q for assigning low mass where reality P lives. Try it in the KL tab.

q: PSI, your model-monitoring drift metric, is essentially…
w: Same log-ratio core, summed in both directions. You've been computing KL in production for years without the name.

q: Log-loss of predicting p = 0.5 on every loan:
w: Any trained model must beat 0.693. A useful sanity anchor when someone quotes log-loss without context.

q: FiLM conditioning applies, per feature:
o: A scale γ(s) and a shift β(s), both computed from a conditioning input s
w: h̃ = γ(s) ⊙ h + β(s). In your paper: s = the macro scenario, h = the loan embedding. Two numbers per feature — the whole trick.

q: LayerNorm normalizes across…
o: The features of a single sample
w: BatchNorm averages over the batch — awkward for variable-length sequences and small batches. Transformers use LayerNorm.

q: Why does LayerNorm keep learnable γ and β after standardizing?
o: So the network can undo or reshape the normalization — restoring expressive power
w: Strict mean-0/std-1 would limit the layer. FiLM is exactly this affine pair, generated dynamically instead of learned as constants.

q: Vanishing gradients arise because…
o: Backprop multiplies many small derivatives (saturated activations) through depth
w: Chain rule = a product of layer derivatives. Many factors < 1 ⇒ exponential decay. Fixes: ReLU, residuals, normalization.

q: An embedding is…
o: A learned dense vector representing a discrete thing — a loan, a word, a token
w: One-hot says 'which one'; an embedding says 'what it's like' — distances become meaningful. Your encoder's h is this, for loans.

q: Residual connections (x + f(x)) help mainly by…
o: Giving gradients a direct path through depth — the identity's derivative is 1
w: Each block learns a correction to identity, not a full mapping. Gradients flow through the '+' untouched — depth stops killing them.

q: High train error AND high test error means…
o: High bias — the model family is too stiff (underfitting)
w: Both errors high = the family can't represent the truth. More data won't fix it; more capacity or better features will.

q: Which penalty drives coefficients to EXACT zero?
o: L1 (lasso) — its diamond constraint has corners on the axes
Neither — only trees do feature selection
w: L2's circular constraint shrinks smoothly but almost never hits zero. L1's corners make sparsity geometric, not accidental.

q: Increasing λ (regularization strength) moves you toward…
w: Regularization deliberately buys bias to sell variance. The optimal λ sits at the bottom of the validation U-curve.

q: Adding more training data primarily helps when…
w: More rows tame a fit that's chasing noise. They cannot make a straight line bend — bias needs capacity or features.

q: In sklearn's LogisticRegression, the parameter C is…
o: INVERSE regularization strength — small C means a strong penalty
w: C = 1/λ. Cranking C to 100 nearly removes the penalty. A classic hands-on gotcha that separates users from readers.

q: Early stopping, dropout, L2, and shallower trees share what role?
o: All are regularizers — trading a little bias for less variance
w: Different mechanisms, one trade. In an interview, naming the shared role before the differences shows structure.

q: AUC = 0.5 means…
o: The ranking is no better than random
w: AUC is P(random positive outranks random negative); 0.5 is coin-flip ranking. It says nothing about calibration either way.

q: Gini (scorecard convention) relates to AUC by…
w: AUC 0.75 ⟺ Gini 0.50. Fintech reports Gini and KS; tech reports AUC — speak both dialects.

q: The KS statistic is…
o: The max gap between the two classes' cumulative score distributions — max(TPR − FPR)
The mean squared error of scores
The area under the PR curve
w: The standard scorecard discrimination stat. Visually: the widest vertical daylight between the goods' and bads' CDFs.

q: A model has AUC 0.95 but is useless for capital calculation because…
o: Its probabilities are uncalibrated — ranking quality says nothing about PD levels
AUC is too high to be real
w: Discrimination and calibration are orthogonal. Capital is computed from PD LEVELS, so a perfect ranker with distorted levels fails validation.

q: With 2% positives, the accuracy of always predicting negative is…
o: 98% — which is why accuracy lies under imbalance
w: The do-nothing model looks excellent. This is the canonical argument for precision/recall and PR-AUC on rare events.

q: AUC is invariant to…
o: Any monotone transform of the scores
Class imbalance changes in the test set
w: Only order matters to AUC. Logistic scores, their logits, or their ranks all give the identical AUC.

q: Gradient boosting fits each new tree to…
o: The negative gradient of the loss w.r.t. current predictions
A bootstrap resample of the data
w: Gradient descent in function space. For squared error the gradient IS the residual; for log-loss it's p − y.

q: Random forests mainly reduce ___; boosting mainly reduces ___:
w: Averaging many deep, decorrelated trees cancels variance. Sequentially stacking weak learners attacks bias. One sentence, top-five question.

q: Feature scaling before XGBoost is…
o: Unnecessary — splits depend only on feature ORDER
w: Monotone transforms leave every split decision unchanged. Scaling matters for gradient-fitted models (ch. 6), not trees.

q: XGBoost handles missing values by…
o: Learning a default direction per split — no imputation needed
w: Each split routes missings to whichever child improved the loss during training. Mentioning this signals hands-on experience.

q: Lowering the learning rate (shrinkage) in GBM typically requires…
o: More trees, and usually generalizes better
w: Small ν + many trees is the robust recipe — tune the pair together with early stopping on a validation set.

q: A single, fully-grown decision tree typically suffers from…
o: High variance — it memorizes the sample
w: Deep trees fit noise; that's why forests average many of them and boosting keeps each one shallow.

q: A p-value is…
o: P(data at least this extreme | the null is true)
P(the null is true | the data)
w: The inversion error is the single most common stats-round failure. The p-value conditions ON the null, never on it being true.

q: Repeatedly checking an A/B test and stopping at first significance…
o: Is fine if n is large
w: Each peek is another lottery ticket against a true null. Fix the horizon in advance or use sequential corrections.

q: Statistical power is…
o: P(rejecting the null | an effect of a given size truly exists)
w: Power drives sample-size planning before launch. Underpowered tests mostly produce unreproducible noise.

q: A 95% CI for a difference contains 0. This means…
o: Not significant at the 5% level (two-sided) — CI/test duality
The test was underpowered for sure
w: Confidence intervals and tests are two views of one machine. Absence of evidence ≠ evidence of absence, though.

q: The bootstrap estimates a statistic's uncertainty by…
o: Resampling your sample with replacement and recomputing the statistic many times
w: When no SE formula exists — for a Gini, a KS, a ratio — the bootstrap manufactures one. Σ-as-a-loop applied to inference.

q: At n = 10 million per arm, a 0.01% lift comes back significant. You should…
o: Check practical significance — huge n makes worthless effects 'significant'
w: Statistical and practical significance diverge at scale. State the minimum effect worth acting on before launching.

q: Your first move in an ML system design interview:
o: Clarify the objective, cost asymmetry, latency, and constraints — before any modeling
w: Five minutes of scoping is graded higher than any architecture. The 30-second opener in chapter 15 is built for this.

q: Defining a default label properly requires…
o: A maturity window — e.g. 90+DPD within 12 months — and excluding immature accounts
Using whatever flag is in the table
w: Recent originations haven't had time to default; including them biases the rate down. Label maturity is the design-round depth check.

q: Temporal (out-of-time) validation exists because…
o: Random splits leak the future, and production always faces drifted data
w: You will score tomorrow's loans with yesterday's model. Your vintage-split instinct from CCAR is exactly this — say it.

q: Shadow mode / champion–challenger means…
o: The new model scores live traffic silently alongside production before any decisions switch
Two models vote on each case
w: It de-risks cutover: you compare distributions and would-be decisions on real traffic with zero customer impact.

q: After deployment, the EARLIEST drift signal is usually…
o: Input/score distribution shift (PSI) — because labels arrive with delay
w: Default labels mature in months; fraud confirms in weeks. Input and score PSI move first — monitor them, not just performance.

q: Adverse action requirements in credit demand…
o: Per-decision explainability — reason codes, e.g. SHAP-derived top factors
w: A declined applicant must be told why. This constrains model choice and is the governance layer most AS candidates have never touched — your edge.

q: Multi-head attention beats single-head primarily because…
o: Heads specialize emergently on different relation types, in parallel subspaces
It removes the need for softmax
w: Each head gets its own Q/K/V projection into a d/h subspace; trained heads end up tracking different dependencies — something one head provably can't.

q: A transformer without positional encoding is…
w: Attention is a weighted average with no notion of position. You ADD a position signal (sinusoidal, learned, or rotary/RoPE) to restore order.

q: Residual connections x + F(x) help because…
o: The identity path has derivative 1, so gradients survive arbitrary depth
w: Each block learns a correction to identity; backprop flows through the + untouched. Without them, 100-layer nets don't train.

q: BERT's masked language modeling trains the model to…
o: Predict hidden tokens from bidirectional context — a self-supervised task
w: Hide ~15% of tokens, predict them. Labels come free from the data itself — that's what lets pretraining use billions of examples.

q: LoRA makes finetuning cheap by…
o: Freezing the model and training small low-rank adapter matrices (<1% of params)
w: ΔW = BA with B, A low-rank. Weight updates are empirically low-rank, so the adapters recover near-full-finetune quality for a fraction of the cost.

q: RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) primarily addresses…
o: Stale knowledge and hallucination — retrieve documents and condition on them at inference
w: Instead of baking facts into weights, fetch relevant docs per query and ground the generation. Adds a dependency on retrieval quality.

q: RLHF aligns an LLM by…
o: Training a reward model on human preference rankings, then optimizing the policy against it
w: Humans rank outputs → reward model → optimize the LLM (PPO, or DPO which skips the separate RL step). Alignment, not capability.

q: The Research Scientist (vs Applied) framing of attention is…
o: What problem RNNs couldn't solve — sequential bottleneck and long-range forgetting
w: RS answers WHY a method exists and what it cost (O(n²)); AS answers how it works. Lineage — problem, idea, tradeoff, next — is the RS tell.

q: The strongest reviewer critique of a paper claiming a gain is usually…
o: Was the baseline tuned as hard as the proposed method?
w: Weakened baselines flatter new methods. After that: does the ablation isolate the claimed mechanism, and is the gain outside noise?

q: An ablation study exists to…
o: Isolate which component caused the gain by removing one at a time
w: Was it the conditioning or just the extra parameters? Ablate FiLM but keep the param count. A result without ablations is a correlation.

q: Causal effect (ATE) differs from correlation because…
o: It compares the do(treat) world to the do(control) world — intervention, not observation
w: You never see both worlds for one unit. Randomization makes treatment independent of confounders; propensity/DiD/IV approximate that when you can't randomize.

q: Difference-in-Differences relies on which assumption?
o: Parallel trends — treated and control would have moved together absent treatment
w: DiD cancels fixed differences by comparing CHANGES around the intervention. The whole method stands or falls on parallel trends — state it.

q: Gini IMPURITY (in CART) and the Gini COEFFICIENT (from AUC) are…
o: Different concepts that share a name
w: Impurity = 1−Σpₖ² measures node purity for splits; the coefficient = 2·AUC−1 measures ranking. Interviewers test this exact confusion.

q: To test whether two models' AUCs differ significantly on the same test set, the standard tool is…
o: DeLong's test — a paired test on the same cases
w: The two AUCs are computed on the SAME examples, so the comparison is paired. DeLong gives the significance; a bootstrap gives a CI on the difference.

q: Concept shift (vs covariate shift) means…
o: The relationship P(y|x) itself changed — e.g. 2020 forbearance broke delinquency→default
w: Covariate shift (P(x)) is caught by PSI; label shift moves P(y); concept shift breaks the mapping itself and silently invalidates the model even if inputs look normal.

q: Pre-norm transformers (norm before each sublayer) are preferred over post-norm because…
o: They keep a clean identity path and train deep stacks stably without delicate LR warmup
They remove the need for residuals
They are required for attention to work
w: Post-norm (the original) puts LayerNorm after the residual add and needs careful warmup. Pre-norm keeps gradients flowing through the identity — why modern LLMs stack dozens of layers.

q: A credit scorecard trained only on APPROVED loans suffers from…
o: Selection bias — reject inference is needed to generalize to all applicants
w: Rejects have no outcome label, so the training set is a censored, non-random slice. Reject inference estimates reject performance to correct toward the through-the-door population.
